title: Tropical Incidence Relations, Polytopes, and Concordant Matroids
overall_summary: The paper defines flag Dressians as moduli spaces of flags of tropical linear spaces and proves tropical incidence relations characterizing when one tropical linear space is contained in another. The central method is tropical duality: complementing Pl├╝cker indices gives an inclusion-reversing bijection on tropical linear spaces, and containment XŌŖéY is converted into membership of dual tropical points in the hyperplanes defining X^ŌŖź. For two-step flags, the paper also gives a polyhedral reformulation: tropical Pl├╝cker plus incidence relations are equivalent to the induced subdivision of Ōłå(p,q;n)=Conv(Ōłå(p,n)Ōł¬Ōłå(q,n)) having the same one-skeleton as Ōłå(p,q;n), with each incidence relation modeled locally by a crosspolytope. For the original problem, the most directly relevant results are the order-reversing tropical duality on ambient tropical linear spaces and the incidence equations that allow inclusion in a tropical linear space to be checked in Pl├╝cker coordinates; the later matroid-concordance discussion may help relate flags of tropical spaces to matroid quotients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1 (tropical duality)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a tropical linear space in R^n/(1,...,1) with tropical Pl├╝cker coordinates x_I indexed by d-subsets IŌŖé[n]. For each (nŌłÆd)-subset JŌŖé[n], define x^ŌŖź_J = x_{[n]ŌłÆJ}. Then the numbers x^ŌŖź_J are tropical Pl├╝cker coordinates of a tropical linear space X^ŌŖź, called the tropical dual linear space or orthogonal complement of X. Moreover, the map XŌå”X^ŌŖź is a bijection on tropical linear spaces, sends rank d spaces to rank nŌłÆd spaces, is involutive, and reverses inclusion: if XŌŖéY then Y^ŌŖźŌŖéX^ŌŖź.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites the remarks preceding Speyer, Proposition 2.12, for this fact. The Pl├╝cker-coordinate part is obtained by observing that the tropical Pl├╝cker relations are invariant under replacing every index set by its complement in [n]. Applying the construction twice returns the original coordinates. The inclusion reversal is the tropical analogue of classical orthogonal-complement duality and is used in the paper by replacing a containment XŌŖéH of a tropical linear space in a tropical hyperplane with the dual containment H^ŌŖźŌŖéX^ŌŖź.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1 (tropical incidence relations)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X and Y be tropical linear spaces in R^n/(1,...,1) with Pl├╝cker coordinates X_I for p-subsets IŌŖé[n] and Y_J for q-subsets JŌŖé[n], with pŌēżq; in the paper's convention, X and Y have tropical dimensions pŌłÆ1 and qŌłÆ1. Then XŌŖéY if and only if, for every S,TŌŖé[n] with |S|=pŌłÆ1 and |T|=q+1, the tropical incidence relation Trop(Ōłæ_{iŌłłTŌłÆS} X_{SŌł¬{i}} Y_{TŌłÆ{i}}) holds, equivalently the minimum of the numbers X_{SŌł¬{i}}+Y_{TŌłÆ{i}} for iŌłłTŌłÆS is attained at least twice. These relations are the tropicalizations of the classical incidence polynomials Ōłæ_{iŌłłTŌłÆS} X_{SŌł¬{i}}Y_{TŌłÆ{i}}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By the definition of a tropical linear space from its Pl├╝cker coordinates, Y is the intersection over all (q+1)-subsets TŌŖé[n] of the tropical hyperplanes H_T = Trop(Ōłæ_{jŌłłT} Y_{TŌłÆ{j}} W_j). Hence XŌŖéY is equivalent to XŌŖéH_T for every such T. Applying Lemma 1, this is equivalent to H_T^ŌŖźŌŖéX^ŌŖź for every T. The dual X^ŌŖź is itself an intersection of tropical hyperplanes indexed by subsets I'ŌŖé[n] of size nŌłÆp+1, with equations Trop(Ōłæ_{r} X^ŌŖź_{I'ŌłÆ{i'_r}} W_{i'_r}). The dual H_T^ŌŖź is represented by a tropical point supported on T with coordinates Y_{TŌłÆ{i}} for iŌłłT, so requiring this point to lie in each defining hyperplane of X^ŌŖź gives the tropical condition Trop(Ōłæ_{iŌłłTŌł®I'} X^ŌŖź_{I'ŌłÆ{i}} Y_{TŌłÆ{i}}). Now set S=[n]ŌłÆI'. Then |S|=pŌłÆ1, TŌł®I'=TŌłÆS, and X^ŌŖź_{I'ŌłÆ{i}}=X_{[n]ŌłÆ(I'ŌłÆ{i})}=X_{SŌł¬{i}}, which gives exactly Trop(Ōłæ_{iŌłłTŌłÆS} X_{SŌł¬{i}}Y_{TŌłÆ{i}}). Reversing these implications proves the converse.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Consequence of Theorem 1 (flag Dressian as a tropical prevariety)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For fixed integers d1<d2<...<ds, the flag Dressian FD(d1,d2,...,ds;n) is the set of tuples (X1,...,Xs) of tropical linear spaces in R^n/(1,...,1) such that XiŌŖéXi+1 for all i. Set-theoretically, membership in FD(d1,...,ds;n) is equivalent to imposing the tropical Pl├╝cker relations on the Pl├╝cker coordinates of each Xi together with the tropical incidence relations of Theorem 1 for each consecutive containment XiŌŖéXi+1. Thus the flag Dressian is an intersection of tropical hypersurfaces, called a tropical prevariety in the paper.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The definition of a tropical linear space supplies the tropical Pl├╝cker relations for each component Xi. Theorem 1 supplies necessary and sufficient tropical equations for each inclusion XiŌŖéXi+1. Imposing these conditions for all adjacent pairs in the flag is equivalent to the full chain of containments, so their common solution set is exactly the flag Dressian.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Speyer proposition quoted in the paper (Proposition 1 in the introduction and Proposition 5 in Section 4)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p_I be real weights assigned to the vertices e_I of the hypersimplex Ōłå(d,n), where I ranges over d-subsets of [n], and let D be the lower subdivision of Ōłå(d,n) induced by these weights. The paper quotes Speyer's equivalence: (1) the numbers p_I are tropical Pl├╝cker coordinates; (2) the one-skeletons of D and Ōłå(d,n) are the same; (3) every face or cell of D is matroidal, meaning its vertices, viewed as d-subsets of [n], are the bases of a matroid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: No proof is given in this paper; it is cited from Speyer, Tropical linear spaces. Haque uses this equivalence as the model for the analogous incidence statement in Theorem 2 and as the source of the matroidal-cell condition for ordinary tropical linear spaces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2 (subdivisions of the crosspolytope P_n)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_n=Conv({┬▒e_i : iŌłł[n]}). Given a function Žł on the vertices of P_n, define Žł_i^+=Žł(e_i), Žł_i^ŌłÆ=Žł(ŌłÆe_i), and w_i=Žł_i^+ + Žł_i^ŌłÆ. After relabeling so that w_1Ōēżw_2Ōēż...Ōēżw_n, the induced lower subdivision contains the segment between the opposite vertices e_1 and ŌłÆe_1 as an edge if and only if w_1<w_2. Moreover, if w_1=w_2, then the subdivision introduces no new edges.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose the affine hyperplane in R^{n+1} through the lifted vertices (ŌłÆe_i,Žł(ŌłÆe_i)) for all i and (e_1,Žł(e_1)). Let L be the corresponding affine linear function on R^n and replace Žł by Žł'=ŽłŌłÆL; this does not change the induced subdivision. By construction Žł'(ŌłÆe_i)=0 for all i and Žł'(e_1)=0, while Žł'(e_i)Ōēź0 for all i, with equality exactly when w_i=w_1. Thus the lower face at height zero contains all ŌłÆe_i and exactly those e_j with w_j=w_1. If w_1 is strictly smaller than all other w_i, the opposite pair e_1,ŌłÆe_1 appears as an edge in the subdivision. If the minimum is attained at least twice, the lower faces have no such new opposite edge, and the proposition concludes that no new edges are introduced.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3 (incidence polytopes are faces of Ōłå(p,q;n), as stated)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Ōłå(d,n)=Conv(e_I : IŌŖé[n], |I|=d), let Ōłå=Ōłå(p,q;n)=Conv(Ōłå(p,n)Ōł¬Ōłå(q,n)), and write e_A=Ōłæ_{aŌłłA}e_a. For S,TŌŖé[n] with |S|=pŌłÆ1 and |T|=q+1, define Ōłå_{S,T}=Conv(e_{SŌł¬{i}}, e_{TŌłÆ{i}} : iŌłłTŌłÆS). The paper states that Ōłå_{S,T} is a face of Ōłå.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof considers the linear functional L(x)=Ōłæ_{iŌłłS}x_iŌłÆŌłæ_{jŌłēT}x_j. On coordinate basis vectors, L(e_j)=1 for jŌłłSŌł®T, L(e_j)=ŌłÆ1 for jŌłēSŌł¬T, and L(e_j)=0 otherwise. Hence L(e_I)Ōēż|SŌł®T| for vertices e_I of Ōłå, and equality occurs only when SŌł®TŌŖéIŌŖéSŌł¬T. The vertices e_{SŌł¬{i}} and e_{TŌłÆ{i}} with iŌłłTŌłÆS all attain this value. The paper then identifies these as exactly the vertices of Ōłå attaining the maximum and concludes that they form an exposed face. This identification is immediate in the common case SŌŖéT; the paper states the proposition for S,T of the displayed sizes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4 (incidence faces are affinely crosspolytopal)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For S,TŌŖé[n] with |S|=pŌłÆ1 and |T|=q+1, the polytope Ōłå_{S,T}=Conv(e_{SŌł¬{i}}, e_{TŌłÆ{i}} : iŌłłTŌłÆS) is affinely equivalent to the crosspolytope P_{|TŌłÆS|}=Conv({┬▒e_i : iŌłłTŌłÆS}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First treat the case S=Ōłģ and T=[m]. Then Ōłå_{Ōłģ,[m]} has opposite vertices e_i and e_{[m]ŌłÆ{i}}. The paper gives an affine map xŌå”Ax+b with A(e_i)=e_iŌłÆ(1/(mŌłÆ2))Ōłæ_{jŌłł[m]}e_j and b=(1/(mŌłÆ2))Ōłæ_{jŌłł[m]}e_j; this sends e_i to e_i and e_{[m]ŌłÆ{i}} to ŌłÆe_i. For general S,T, first apply the affine map that kills the coordinates indexed by S and keeps the other coordinates: on vertices it sends e_{SŌł¬{i}} to e_i and e_{TŌłÆ{i}} to e_{(TŌłÆS)ŌłÆ{i}}. This identifies Ōłå_{S,T} with the S=Ōłģ case for the ground set TŌłÆS, and composing with the previous affine map gives the desired equivalence to P_{|TŌłÆS|}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1 (one incidence relation equals no new edge on the corresponding crosspolytope)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let D be the subdivision of Ōłå_{S,T} induced by weights x_I on p-subset vertices and y_J on q-subset vertices. Then D and Ōłå_{S,T} have the same one-skeleton if and only if the tropical incidence relation Trop(Ōłæ_{iŌłłTŌłÆS} X_{SŌł¬{i}}Y_{TŌłÆ{i}}) holds, equivalently the minimum of x_{SŌł¬{i}}+y_{TŌłÆ{i}} over iŌłłTŌłÆS is attained at least twice.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Proposition 4 to identify Ōłå_{S,T} affinely with the crosspolytope P_{|TŌłÆS|}. Under this identification, each pair of vertices e_{SŌł¬{i}} and e_{TŌłÆ{i}} becomes an opposite pair e_j and ŌłÆe_j. The quantity w_j of Proposition 2 is exactly x_{SŌł¬{i}}+y_{TŌłÆ{i}}, which is the tropical weight of the corresponding term in the incidence relation. Proposition 2 says that a new opposite edge is introduced precisely when this w_j is the unique minimum. Therefore the subdivision has no new edge, equivalently the same one-skeleton, precisely when the minimum in the incidence relation is not unique.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2 (mixed edges of Ōłå(p,q;n))
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I,JŌŖé[n] with |I|=p and |J|=q, and let Ōłå=Ōłå(p,q;n)=Conv(Ōłå(p,n)Ōł¬Ōłå(q,n)). The following are equivalent: (1) the segment e_Ie_J is an edge of Ōłå; (2) the monomial X_IY_J appears in no tropical incidence relation Trop(Ōłæ_{iŌłłTŌłÆS} X_{SŌł¬{i}}Y_{TŌłÆ{i}}); (3) IŌŖéJ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1) implies (2), the paper proves the contrapositive. If X_IY_J appears in the incidence relation for S,T, then I=SŌł¬{i} and J=TŌłÆ{i} for some iŌłłTŌłÆS. By Proposition 4 these two vertices are opposite vertices in the crosspolytope Ōłå_{S,T}, hence they are not joined by an edge of Ōłå_{S,T}; since Ōłå_{S,T} is a face of Ōłå by Proposition 3, they cannot form an edge of Ōłå. For (2) implies (3), if I is not contained in J, choose rŌłłIŌłÆJ, set S=IŌłÆ{r} and T=JŌł¬{r}; then X_IY_J is a term in the incidence relation indexed by S,T. For (3) implies (1), define L(x)=Ōłæ_{iŌłłI}x_iŌłÆŌłæ_{jŌłēJ}x_j. On vertices of Ōłå one has LŌēżp, and equality occurs only for the p-vertex e_I and the q-vertex e_J when IŌŖéJ. Thus the hyperplane L=p exposes exactly the segment e_Ie_J, making it an edge.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2 (polyhedral characterization of tropical incidence prevarieties)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let x_I for |I|=p and y_J for |J|=q be real weights on the vertices of Ōłå(p,n) and Ōłå(q,n), and let D be the lower subdivision of Ōłå(p,q;n)=Conv(Ōłå(p,n)Ōł¬Ōłå(q,n)) induced by these weights. The following are equivalent: (1) the collections x_I and y_J satisfy the tropical Pl├╝cker relations of ranks p and q, respectively, and together satisfy all tropical incidence relations Trop(Ōłæ_{iŌłłTŌłÆS} X_{SŌł¬{i}}Y_{TŌłÆ{i}}); (2) the subdivision D and the polytope Ōłå(p,q;n) have the same one-skeleton.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The intended argument combines Speyer's hypersimplex criterion with Corollary 1 and Lemma 2. If the tropical Pl├╝cker relations hold for x and y separately, then by Speyer's proposition the restrictions of D to Ōłå(p,n) and Ōłå(q,n) introduce no new pure p-p or q-q edges. Suppose a mixed edge e_Ie_J appears in D but is not an edge of Ōłå(p,q;n). By Lemma 2, this means I is not contained in J, and the monomial X_IY_J occurs in some incidence relation indexed by S,T. The corresponding polytope Ōłå_{S,T} contains e_I and e_J as an opposite pair. Corollary 1 says that satisfaction of the incidence relation prevents such a new opposite edge in the restricted subdivision, a contradiction. Conversely, if D and Ōłå have the same one-skeleton, then the restrictions to the two hypersimplices have the same one-skeleton, so Speyer's proposition gives the two sets of tropical Pl├╝cker relations. Also, for every S,T, the restriction of D to Ōłå_{S,T} has the same one-skeleton as Ōłå_{S,T}, so Corollary 1 gives the corresponding tropical incidence relation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 5 / White Proposition 7.4.7(1) quoted (matroid quotient or concordance)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and M' be matroids on the same ground set E, with base sets B(M) and B(M'). The paper uses the following basis criterion from White as the definition of M' being a quotient of M, or equivalently M' and M being concordant: for every BŌłłB(M) and every iŌłłEŌłÆB, there exists B'ŌłłB(M') with B'ŌŖéB such that {j : (BŌł¬{i})ŌłÆ{j}ŌłłB(M)} contains {j : (B'Ōł¬{i})ŌłÆ{j}ŌłłB(M')}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not prove this criterion; it cites White, Theory of matroids, Proposition 7.4.7. The displayed containment says that whenever an element i can be exchanged into the smaller-rank basis B' of M' by removing j, the same exchange is available from the containing basis B of M. The paper uses this as the matroidal relation expected to model containment of the corresponding tropical linear spaces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 1 quoted from White (vector matroid quotients)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For vector matroids, matroid quotients correspond to quotient vector spaces. If M is represented by a set V' of vectors in a vector space V, then a quotient M' is represented by the images {v+W : vŌłłV'} in V/W for some subspace W. In particular, every vectorial nullity-k quotient MŌåÆM' can be realized by choosing a matrix representation of M that becomes a matrix representation of M' after deleting its first k rows.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this as White, Proposition 7.4.8(2), without proof. The conceptual reason is that projecting a vector configuration to a quotient vector space can only add linear dependencies, which is exactly the strong-map or quotient behavior for matroids; deleting rows in a matrix representation realizes such a projection in coordinates.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Observation in Section 4 (realizable flags have concordant cells)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be the field of Puiseaux series in the variable t. Suppose VŌŖéWŌŖéK^n are vector spaces with dim V=p and dim W=q, and let X_I and Y_J be the tropical Pl├╝cker coordinates of Trop(V) and Trop(W). Let D be the subdivision of Ōłå(p,q;n) induced by these coordinates. Then every cell Q of D is a concordant polytope: if F_p and F_q are the p- and q-dimensional hypersimplex parts of Q, then the matroids whose bases are the p-subsets and q-subsets labeling the vertices of F_p and F_q are concordant in the sense of the matroid quotient relation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a cell Q of D. By the definition of a regular subdivision, there is a linear functional L such that L(e_I)=X_I for p-vertices e_I in Q and L(e_I)>X_I for p-vertices outside Q, and similarly L(e_J)=Y_J on q-vertices in Q and L(e_J)>Y_J outside Q. Let a_R=L(e_R). Because L is linear, these a_R are additive in R, so one can diagonally rescale coordinates over K by sending e_R to t^{ŌłÆa_R}e_R on exterior powers. Replacing V and W by their rescaled versions shifts the tropical Pl├╝cker coordinates to X_IŌłÆL(e_I) and Y_JŌłÆL(e_J), which are zero exactly on the vertices of Q and positive outside. Reducing the rescaled vector spaces modulo t gives vector spaces over the residue field whose nonzero Pl├╝cker coordinates are precisely the vertices in Q. For a p-plane V, the Ith Pl├╝cker coordinate is nonzero exactly when the coordinate vectors indexed by [n]ŌłÆI form a basis of K^n/V, and similarly for W. Since VŌŖéW, there is a quotient map K^n/VŌåÆK^n/W, so the matroid from the complements of the q-vertices is a quotient of the matroid from the complements of the p-vertices. Taking complements of bases gives concordance for the matroids attached to the p- and q-vertices of Q.

proof_techniques.name: Complement-index tropical duality
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the operation x_IŌå”x_{[n]ŌłÆI} on Pl├╝cker coordinates to pass from a tropical linear space X to its tropical orthogonal complement X^ŌŖź. This operation is involutive, sends rank d to rank nŌłÆd, and reverses inclusion of tropical linear spaces. It is the tropical replacement for classical orthogonal complement duality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the full ambient Dressian, this already gives an order-reversing involution on all tropical linear spaces. In the relative Dressian problem, the key test would be whether an analogous or modified duality preserves the subposet of tropical linear spaces contained in Trop(M) and agrees with the prescribed flat involution.

proof_techniques.name: Deriving incidence by dualizing containment in hyperplanes
proof_techniques.description: To prove the tropical incidence relations, the paper writes Y as the intersection of its defining tropical hyperplanes H_T. The containment XŌŖéY is equivalent to XŌŖéH_T for every T; tropical duality turns this into H_T^ŌŖźŌŖéX^ŌŖź. Substituting the Pl├╝cker coordinates of the dual point H_T^ŌŖź into the hyperplane equations defining X^ŌŖź produces the explicit relations min_i(X_{SŌł¬{i}}+Y_{TŌłÆ{i}}) attained at least twice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a concrete way to convert inclusions in a poset of tropical linear spaces into coordinate equations. A proposed order-reversing map on a relative Dressian could be checked by verifying that it transforms these incidence equations for XŌŖéY into the corresponding equations for ╬”(Y)ŌŖé╬”(X).

proof_techniques.name: Flag Dressian as a Pl├╝cker-incidence prevariety
proof_techniques.description: The flag Dressian is described set-theoretically by imposing ordinary tropical Pl├╝cker relations on each component and tropical incidence relations on consecutive components. This description does not require realizability of the tropical linear spaces and is therefore genuinely Dressian-level rather than Grassmannian-level.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The relative Dressian Dr(M) can be approached similarly as a moduli/poset cut out by containment conditions. The paper's equations suggest studying automorphisms or anti-automorphisms of Dr(M) through their action on Pl├╝cker data and incidence relations.

proof_techniques.name: Crosspolytope local model for one incidence relation
proof_techniques.description: For each incidence relation indexed by S,T, the paper associates a polytope Ōłå_{S,T} whose vertices are the monomials in that relation. This polytope is affinely equivalent to a crosspolytope, with each term X_{SŌł¬{i}}Y_{TŌłÆ{i}} corresponding to a pair of opposite vertices. The tropical incidence condition is exactly the assertion that the minimum of the sums of weights on opposite pairs is not unique, which is equivalent to the induced subdivision not creating a new opposite edge.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If containment in Trop(M) or between elements of Dr(M) is hard to check globally, this technique suggests reducing it to small local crosspolytope tests. Such local tests may be useful for proving that a candidate involution preserves or reverses containment.

proof_techniques.name: One-skeleton criterion for two-step tropical flags
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 2 packages the tropical Pl├╝cker relations for two spaces and all incidence relations between them into a single regular-subdivision condition: the induced subdivision of Ōłå(p,q;n)=Conv(Ōłå(p,n)Ōł¬Ōłå(q,n)) has the same one-skeleton as the original polytope. Mixed edges of Ōłå correspond exactly to subset containments IŌŖéJ between p- and q-subsets, while forbidden mixed edges correspond to monomials appearing in incidence relations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution extending a flat involution might be studied combinatorially by asking whether it induces a symmetry or anti-symmetry of the relevant subdivision/edge data. The subset-containment characterization of mixed edges is particularly close to lattice-order behavior.

proof_techniques.name: Reduction to the case p+qŌēżn by duality
proof_techniques.description: In the polytope section, the paper assumes p+qŌēżn, explaining that when p+qŌēźn one can dualize the incidence variety to the type (nŌłÆq,nŌłÆp), where the sum of the new ranks is at most n. The number of equations and the number of terms in the equations are preserved under this dualization.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a useful dimension-reversal normalization. In a relative setting, it suggests that any construction of ╬” should be compatible with swapping a subspace dimension with its complementary dimension.

proof_techniques.name: Initial degeneration of realizable flags to matroid quotients
proof_techniques.description: For a realizable flag VŌŖéW over Puiseaux series, each cell of the induced subdivision can be isolated by a linear functional. Diagonal rescaling by the corresponding weights and reduction modulo t turns the cell into the support of nonzero Pl├╝cker coordinates of an initial flag over the residue field. The inclusion of vector spaces then yields a quotient relation between the associated vector matroids, giving concordant matroids attached to the cell.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is matroidal, and this technique connects tropical containment data to matroid quotient data on cells. It may help analyze how the flat involution interacts with contractions, quotients, or initial matroids arising from elements of Dr(M).

other_useful_info: The paper defines FD(d1,...,ds;n) as the set of flags (X1,...,Xs) of tropical linear spaces in R^n/(1,...,1) with XiŌŖéXi+1; the two-step case FD(p,q;n) is also called a tropical incidence prevariety. It explicitly distinguishes FD from the tropicalization of the classical flag variety: in FD the individual tropical linear spaces need not be realizable, and even realizability of the two spaces need not automatically imply realizability of the containment. In Section 4 the paper introduces concordant polytopes Conv(F,G), where F and G are matroidal polytopes and their associated matroids are concordant in the matroid quotient sense. It asks whether cells of subdivisions coming from FD(p,q;n) are concordant polytopes and whether a converse holds; it proves this for realizable flags via the Observation above. The paper also records Example 1 in Ōłå(2,3;4): the polytope with p-side bases {12,13,24,34} and q-side bases {123,124,134} lies in the one-skeleton but the two matroids are not concordant; the example is used to motivate the stronger subdivision-level conjecture that, under the Pl├╝cker and incidence relations, a cell has no internal edges if and only if it is concordant. Main external references used by the paper are Speyer, Tropical linear spaces, for tropical Pl├╝cker vectors, duality, and matroidal subdivisions, and White, Theory of matroids, for matroid quotients/concordance.
why_relevant: Develops tropical incidence relations characterizing containment of one tropical linear space in another. This is foundational for translating inclusion in Dr(M) into valuated-matroid or polyhedral conditions.
